Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


000
001
      D ISCOVERING P HYSICS L AWS OF DYNAMICAL S YS -
002   TEMS VIA I NVARIANT F UNCTION L EARNING
003
004
005    Anonymous authors
006    Paper under double-blind review
007
008
                                                  A BSTRACT
009
010            We consider learning underlying laws of dynamical systems governed by ordi-
011            nary differential equations (ODE). A key challenge is how to discover intrinsic
012            dynamics across multiple environments while circumventing environment-specific
013            mechanisms. Unlike prior work, we tackle more complex environments where
014            changes extend beyond function coefficients to entirely different function forms.
015            For example, we demonstrate the discovery of ideal pendulum’s natural motion
016            α2 sin θt by observing pendulum dynamics in different environments, such as the
017            damped environment α2 sin(θt ) − ρωt and powered environment α2 sin(θt ) + ρ |ωωtt | .
018            Here, we formulate this problem as an invariant function learning task and pro-
019
               pose a new method, known as Disentanglement of Invariant Functions (DIF),
               that is grounded in causal analysis. We propose a causal graph and design an
020
               encoder-decoder hypernetwork that explicitly disentangles invariant functions from
021
               environment-specific dynamics. The discovery of invariant functions is guaran-
022            teed by our information-based principle that enforces the independence between
023            extracted invariant functions and environments. Quantitative comparisons with
024            meta-learning and invariant learning baselines on three ODE systems demonstrate
025            the effectiveness and efficiency of our method. Furthermore, symbolic regression
026            explanation results highlight the ability of our framework to uncover intrinsic laws.
027
028   1   I NTRODUCTION
029
030   Deep neural networks (Goodfellow et al., 2016) have been widely used for predicting dynamical
031
      systems (Aussem, 1999; Singh et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2016; Lusch et al., 2018; Yeo & Melnyk, 2019;
      Giannakis, 2019). Numerous efforts (Kirchmeyer et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022; Mouli et al., 2024)
032
      have focused on modeling dynamical systems by forecasting future states from past observations,
033
      often emphasizing rapid adaptation to new systems or improved model architectures. However, an
034   important scenario in scientific discovery has been overlooked, i.e., learning invariant mechanisms,
035   which aims to identify shared motion patterns across dynamical systems observed in multiple
036   environments. This task not only facilitates scientific equation discovery but also holds potential
037   for advancing the understanding and extraction of physical laws from observational data—such as
038   images and videos—where physical laws are highly entangled. As the first step in invariant function
039   learning, this paper focuses on ordinary differential equation (ODE) systems.
040   The need for invariant function learning arises because data collected is often observed under
041   varying environments and entangled with multiple factors. For instance, the oscillation of a simple
042   pendulum (Yin et al., 2021b) is commonly influenced by air friction; a prey-predator system (Ahmad,
043   1993) can be affected by limited resources. These factors significantly hinder deep models from
044   learning the true and invariant dynamics. Instead of capturing invariant dynamical patterns, deep
045   models tend to be sensitive to trivial information and spurious correlations, leading to failures in
046   identifying the true and isolated mechanisms. In light of this challenge, we explore an innovative
047   setting called invariant function learning, which aims to extract intrinsic mechanisms from data
048
      observed in multiple environments. Unlike prior work, we aim to tackle broader and more complex
      environments where changes extend beyond function coefficients to entirely different function forms.
049
      For example, we target the discovery of ideal pendulum’s natural motion α2 sin θt by observing
050
      pendulum dynamics in different environments such as the damped environment α2 sin(θt ) − ρωt and
051   powered environment α2 sin(θt ) + ρ |ωωtt | , as shown in our motivation example 1.
052
053   Invariant function learning presents two key challenges. Firstly, invariant mechanisms are not isolated
      entities, and being intertwined with varying initial conditions, system parameters, and time makes

067
068
      Figure 1: Multi-environment Pendulum ODE systems. In this example, ODEs with different
      coefficients and function forms are used to extract their corresponding invariant functions (green).
069
070   them difficult to define or disentangle. Secondly, existing invariant learning techniques (Arjovsky
071   et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2021a; Rosenfeld et al., 2020; Peters et al., 2016), which are primarily designed
072   for categorical tasks, do not extend effortlessly to dynamical systems, requiring the design of new
073   invariant principles. To overcome these challenges, we formulate our invariant function learning
074
      framework as a causal graph where functions are parameterized and isolated to be learned and disen-
      tangled. Furthermore, we propose an invariant function learning principle and the implementation
075
      of an encoder-decoder hypernetwork (Ha et al., 2016) to identify the true invariant mechanism.
076
      Specifically, our contributions are listed as follows. (1) We introduce a new task, invariant function
077   learning, aimed at scientific discovery. (2) We formulate its framework with causal foundations. (3)
078   We propose an invariant function learning principle to identify true invariant mechanisms and design
079   a method, Disentanglement of Invariant Function (DIF), with hypernetwork implementation. (4)
080   To facilitate comprehensive benchmarking, we design several new baselines by adapting existing
081   meta-learning and invariant learning techniques to our function learning framework and conducting
082   extensive experimental studies.
083
084   2     I NVARIANT F UNCTION L EARNING FOR DYNAMICAL S YSTEM
085
086   In this section, we first provide the background on ODEs, followed by the introduction and formulation
087   of our invariant function learning task, along with the causal analyses that underpin our proposal.
088
089   2.1   O RDINARY D IFFERENTIAL E QUATION DYNAMICAL S YSTEM

094   where xt ∈ X ⊆ Rd includes d hidden states of the system at time t. f ∈ F : X 7→ T X is the
095   derivative function of the dynamical system mapping the hidden states to their tangent space, where
096   F is the function space containing functions that describe all dynamical systems with d hidden states.
097
      Given proper time discretization, we consider T time steps denoted as t = t0 , t1 , . . . , tT −1 . The corre-
098   sponding T -length trajectory can be written as a matrix X = [xt0 , xt1 , . . . , xtT −1 ] ∈ Rd×T . Given the
099   system hidden states before a certain time step Tc ∈ N, denoted as Xp = X:,0:Tc = [xt0 , . . . , xtTc −1 ] ∈
100   Rd×Tc , the forecasting task aims to predict the future trajectory X:,Tc :T = [xtTc , . . . , xtT −1 ]. For
101   theoretical analysis, we represent random variables in boldface, e.g., the matrix-valued random
102   variable corresponding to X is denoted as X; the function f is a realization of the function variable f
103   from a given function space. Full notations are detailed in Tab. 3.
104
105   2.2   I NVARIANT F UNCTION L EARNING
106
107   In this paper, we introduce a new task, invariant function learning (IFL). Specifically, given the
      prior distribution of trajectories, denoted as p(X), we consider a scenario where trajectories are

115
116
117
118   observed under multiple environments. The trajectories observed in an environment e ∈ E are
119   sampled from the conditional distribution p(X|e = e). Given a trajectory X from environment e, our
120
      goal is to discover its invariant function fc , which generates the invariant trajectory X c . fc and X c
      only include the shared mechanisms across all environments, thus capturing the underlying natural
121
      laws unaffected by environmental factors. For instance, as illustrated in Fig. 1, in the case of a
122
      pendulum system with varying environmental effects such as frictions or power, the goal is to extract
123   the natural motion of an ideal pendulum when excluding these external influences. A set of specific
124   examples for the task is provided below, more examples are available in Appx. C.
125
126
      Function environments. The environments in this paper are different from those defined in
      CoDA (Kirchmeyer et al., 2022), LEAD (Yin et al., 2021a), and MetaphysiCa (Mouli et al., 2024).
127
      As shown in Tab. 1, the environments/tasks used in previous works, namely, coefficient environments,
128
      are defined by the changes on the function coefficients α and ρ, i.e., each environment contains only
129   one function while different environments include functions with different coefficients. In contrast,
130   we consider more complex cases and define environments as the interventions on function forms, i.e.,
131   each environment can contain functions with the same function form and different coefficients, while
132   different environments differ in function forms. Specifically, in Tab. 1, while coefficient environment
133   1 consists of a single function f1 , our function environment 1 includes all the functions in form of
134   −α2 sin(θt ) − ρωt where α ∼ p(α), ρ ∼ p(ρ). Here, we model these functions as a function random
135   variable f1 .
136   Challenges. However, extracting such invariant dynamics presents two significant challenges. Firstly,
137   invariant mechanisms are intertwined with varying initial conditions, system parameters, and time,
138   making them particularly difficult to isolate or define. Secondly, in dynamical systems, state values
139   and their derivatives evolve over time, meaning that there is no single invariant representation fixed
140   across time steps. This requires defining invariant factors in a function space, where functions can
141   cover dynamical states. Conventional invariant learning techniques are not directly applicable in this
142   context, as they are typically not designed to capture invariance in function spaces. These challenges
143
      necessitate the development of new function representations and the development of a novel invariant
      learning principle tailored to dynamical systems.
144
145
146
      2.2.1   C AUSALITY- BASED D EFINITIONS
147
148
      First challenge: causal formulation. In light of the first challenge, we aim to formulate the invariant
149
      function learning and the dynamical system forecasting problem from a causal perspective. As
150   shown in Fig. 2, we formulate the trajectory data generation process as a Structural Causal Model
151   (SCM) (Pearl, 2009), where c, e, and X0 are exogenous variables. All endogenous variables, except
152   the function composition step fc , fe → f, are generated with extra random noises to model complex
153   real-world scenarios. Please refer to Appx. B.1 for more details. The optimization goal of the
154   forecasting task is to estimate the true distribution p(X). As can be observed from the causal graph,
155   our function learning framework can be considered as two phases, namely, function prediction and
156   forecasting. For the function prediction phase, similar to inverse problems (Lu et al., 2021b), given
157   the observed trajectory Xp , the target is to reversely infer the invariant derivative function f that can
158
      represent the dynamics of the system, i.e., fitting p(f|Xp ). Intuitively, taking Fig. 1 as an example, this
      phase aims at the reasoning of the function basis sin(θt ), −ωt , |ωωtt | , and the coefficients α, ρ. After
159
160   obtaining the derivative function f̂, the forecasting phase feeds it into a numerical integrator with the
161   initial condition X0 for the X forecasting, which can be demonstrated as p(X|f̂, X0 ). Note that the
      bold font variables are random variables instead of individual realizations.


                                                                    3
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

                                                                         e                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     fe
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               <latexit sha1_base64="zvAOh4LPO70ZgKOY62365OcWLOE="></latexit>


                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     X
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     <latexit sha1_base64="21CSHYTtNXl+BWHMKn2BZTOBKfY="></latexit>


                                                                         <latexit sha1_base64="IDLvEKSy6/Ua/W7NVzsaPpXBgTw="></latexit>


169   function, allowing us to isolate the function prediction
170   process and disentangle the learning of invariant functions.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                Function generation                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            Trajectory generation

171
      In the invariant function learning problem, we decompose                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       (Forecasting)
      the exposed function variable f into an invariant function
172                                                                    Figure 2: Structural causal model. The
      variable fc and an environment-specific function variable
173                                                                    causal data generation process includes
      fe , as in Fig. 2. These two functions are caused by the
174   exogenous factors c and e, respectively. Intuitively, the c      two phases: function generation and tra-
175   variable includes the common and invariant mechanism,            jectory generation, which correspond to
176   while e is the environment variable determined by the            our two learning phases in parentheses,
177   observational environment of the trajectory, e.g., the pen-      namely, function prediction and forecast-
178   dulum dynamics can be observed in different mediums              ing. The gray nodes in the causal graph
179   (environments), such as air and water.                           indicate observable variables.
180   Formally, the invariant function learning target is the dis-
181   covery of fc , which eliminates the effect of environments and obtains the invariant mechanism.
182   However, from the d-separation perspective (Pearl, 2009), since Xp is the descendent of the collider f
183   of fc and fe , given Xp , fc and fe are correlated/biased and not distinguishable, preventing the direct fit-
184   ting of fc given Xp . Therefore, we aim to identify this intermediate hidden variable by characterizing
185   the unique properties of the prior distribution of p(fc ). Theoretically, since f is the collider between fc
186   and e, fc is expected to be independent of e. In addition, among all the functions that are independent
187   of e, fc should be the most informative with respect to the observed trajectories, which will be proved
188
      in Thm. 3.1. This forms the foundation of invariant function learning.
189
190
      3     A METHOD : D ISENTANGLEMENT OF I NVARIANT F UNCTION
191
192
193
      Following the two-phase function learning framework, we now propose the first method for IFL,
      Disentanglement of Invariant Function (DIF), with hypernetwork-based implementations of the two
194
      corresponding networks. For the forecasting network, similar to traditional representation learning
195
      tasks, we aim to learn a function f ∈ F : Rd 7→ Rd . For the function prediction, however, it requires
196   learning a function that returns a function, h ∈ H : Rd×Tc 7→ F , i.e., learning a hyper-function, which
197   is enabled using a hypernetwork.
198
199
200
      3.1   H YPERNETWORK D ESIGN
201
      Function prediction. To quantify the objective of the hyper-function, we approximate its output
202
      function as a neural network with m parameters. The function space F consists of all possible
203
      neural networks with m parameters, and a function f ∈ F can be represented as a vector in Rm .
204   Thus this parameterization process introduces a hypernetwork structure (Ha et al., 2016) into the
205   implementation, as shown in Fig. 3. Note that since our parameterization transfers functions into the
206   real number space, it is now possible to apply invariant learning techniques such as IRM (Arjovsky
207   et al., 2019) and VREx (Krueger et al., 2021), where invariant (function) representations need to be
208   extracted. In the following sections, we use F and omit Rm for simplicity.
209
      Practically, since the number of parameters in a network is generally large, we propose to compress
210   the invariant function representations into hidden representations, thus forming an encoder-decoder
211   framework. Specifically, as shown in the Fig. 3, the trajectory encoder is a transformer-based network
212   with positional embedding design, denoted as hθenc : Rd×Tc 7→ Z . Given the hidden representation
213   from the encoder, we further encode an invariant function embedding ẑc ∈ Z and an environment
214   function embedding ẑe ∈ Z using two multilayer perceptrons (MLPs), denoted as hθinv and hθenv ,
215   respectively. Then, aligning with our causal graph as Fig. 2, we combine ẑc and ẑe by summing them
      as the function representation ẑ ∈ Z , which can be used for full dynamics prediction. Finally, we

      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

229   To facilitate theoretical analysis, we simplify the notations and denote the function prediction process
230   in the hypernetwork as two functions fˆ = hθ (Xp ) and fˆc = hθc (Xp ), where hθ , hθc : Rd×Tc 7→ F ;
231   θ = {θenc , θinv , θenv , θdec }; θc = {θenc , θinv , θdec }. In addition, we slightly abuse the notations of hθ
232   and hθc on random variables Xp for simplicity, producing f̂ = hθ (Xp ) and f̂c = hθc (Xp ), respectively.
233   Forecasting. Given the produced neural network function fˆ, we apply a numerical integrator as our
234   forecastor, a function gint that takes a derivative function fˆ and initial states
                                                                                       X0 as inputs, to obtain
235   X̂ = gint (fˆ, X0 )+ϵ where ϵ is sampled from a Gaussian noise N X; 0, σ 2 I introduced by calculation
236   deviations. This forecasting formulation enables the following probability  modeling, where we
237   obtain the forecasting given realizations X0 and fˆ as a Gaussian distribution N X; gint (fˆ, X0 ), σ 2 I
238   denoted as p(X|fˆ, X0 ). Therefore, in probability modeling, X̂ is sampled from p(X|fˆ, X0 ). It is worth
239   noting that unlike in inference time and analyses, it is time-consuming to use numerical integrators
240   during training; therefore, we follow Mouli et al. (2024) and train the model by fitting the derivative
241   dXˆ t = fˆ(X ) with numerical derivatives from the ground-truth X instead. For simplicity, we denote
       dt          t
242   f (·) as a neural network based derivative function with parameters fˆ ∈ Rm .

      3.2     D ISCOVERY OF I NVARIANT F UNCTION
245
246   With the above hypernetwork design, we can now propose the discovery of the invariant function
247   fc . Following the independence and information properties of fc discussed in Sec. 2.2.1, we achieve
248   invariant function learning with the following theorem, which is our main theoretical result.
249   Theorem 3.1 (Invariant function learning principle). Given the causal graph in Fig. 2, and the
250   predicted function random variable f̂c = hθc (Xp ), it follows that the true invariant function random
251   variable fc = hθc∗ (Xp ), where θc∗ is the solution of the following optimization process, described as
252
253                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           θc∗ = arg max I(hθc (Xp ); f|X0 ) s.t. hθc (Xp ) ⊥
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               ⊥ e, 254
255   where I(·; ·) is mutual information that measures the information overlap between the predicted
256   invariant function random variable hθc (Xp ) and the true full-dynamics function random variable f.
257
      The proof is available in Appx. B.2. Thm. 3.1 establish guarantees and conditions for the function
258
      output f̂c of the hypernetwork to be the invariant function fc , fulfilling the goal of the IFL task.
259
260
      3.2.1                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                I MPLEMENTATION OF INVARIANT FUNCTION LEARNING PRINCIPLE
261
262   Following Thm. 3.1, next we introduce the implementation and optimization process of our proposed
263   networks. We first train the encoder and decoder of our hypernetwork by approximating the trajectory
264   distribution p(X), parameterized as p(X|hθ (Xp ), X0 ), where we apply the cross-entropy minimization.
265   Given that our supervision signals only come from the ground-truth trajectories, we introduce a
266   simple lemma for our optimization processes. The proof is provided in Appx. B.3.
267   Lemma 3.2 (ODE cross-entropy minimization). Given forecasting model p(X|hθ (Xp ), X0 ), it fol-
268   lows that the cross-entropy minimization between the data distribution p(X) and p(X|hθ (Xp ), X0 )
269   is equivalent to minimizing mean square error minθ EX∼p ∥X − X̂∥22 , where X̂ is sampled from
      p(X|hθ (Xp ), X0 ).


                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              5
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


270
      In order to discover invariant functions, we apply the invariant function learning principle, which
271   requires maximizing the conditional mutual information between the predicted function random
272   variable f̂c = hθc (Xp ) and f. Based on the derivation of Lemma 3.2, we have the following proposition.
273
      Proposition 3.3 (ODE conditional mutual information maximization). Given forecasting
274
      model p(X|hθc (Xp ), X0 ), it follows that the conditional mutual information maximization
275
      maxθc I(hθc (Xp ); f|X0 ) is equivalent to minimizing mean square error minθc EX∼P ∥X − X̂ c ∥22 , where
276   X̂ c is the predicted trajectory sampled from p(X|hθc (Xp ), X0 ).
277
278   The proof is provided in Appx. B.4. Lemma 3.2 and Prop. 3.3 essentially transfers the mutual
279   information maximization of Thm. 3.1 into an implementable optimization of mean square error
280   (MSE) loss, enabling the practical use of the invariant function learning principle. In addition,
281   the independence constraint in Thm. 3.1 requires that the extracted functions should not be over-
282   informative or contain biased information from environments e. This independence constraint can be
283   implemented in an adversarial way, where we require the environment prediction P (e|f̂c ) to be as
284   less informative as possible, i.e., minimizing the mutual information of I(e; f̂c ). Thus we introduce
285   an environment discriminator gϕ , a.k.a., Pϕ (e|f̂), which aims to distinguish the environment of any
286   function from F . The hypernetwork is trained adversarially to enforce f̂c as indistinguishable as
287   possible. The theoretically analysis of this independence training is provided in Appx. B.5.
288   Objective. The overall optimization objective can be obtained with three training strategies. First,
289   the training of the discriminator is conducted on both fˆc and fˆe to fully capture environment patterns.
290   Second, we use the corresponding hidden representations of fˆc and fˆe , ẑc and ẑe , as the input of the
291   discriminator. Third, as we mentioned in Sec.3.1, during training, we fit derivatives instead of using
292   an integrator.
293
                      X dXt               2                X dXt                    2
294         min EX∼p          − fˆ(Xt ) + λc · min EX∼p                 − fˆc (Xt )
295          θ
                      t
                           dt             2
                                                   θc
                                                             t
                                                                    dt              2
                                                                                                             (3)

            + λdis · min −EX∼p log gϕ (ẑc ) + min −EX∼p log gϕ (ẑe ) + λadv · max −EX∼p log gϕ (ẑc )
                        ϕ                       ϕ,θ̄e                               θ̄c
298
299   where we denote θ̄e = {θenc , θenv }; θ̄c = {θenc , θinv }. Please refer to Appx. D.2.2 for more details.
300   Efficient hypernetwork implementation. Last but not least, one of the major challenges that limits
301   the usage of hypernetworks is the implementation complexity. In this work, we propose a reference-
302   based hypernetwork implementation to accelerate the running speed using only PyTorch Paszke
303   et al. (2019) without re-implementing basic neural networks. The speedup compared to the naïve
304   implementation and the vectorized functional implementation are 16.8x and 2x, respectively. Please
305   refer to Appx. F for implementation and experimental details.
306
307   4    R ELATED W ORK
308
309   This work is inspired by the ideas and limitations of previous research in dynamical system forecasting,
310   meta-learning, and invariant learning.
311
      Deep learning models are widely applied in many physical applications (Lusch et al., 2018; Yeo &
312   Melnyk, 2019; Kochkov et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2018) including partial differential equations (PDEs)
313   with the focus on the multi-scale (Li et al., 2020; Stachenfeld et al., 2021), multi-resolution (Kochkov
314   et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2022), and long-term stability (Li et al., 2021; Lippe et al., 2023) issues.
315   Operator learning and neural operators (Gupta et al., 2021; Kovachki et al., 2023) are popular for
316   PDE estimations. Although the ODE dynamical system does not contain the multi-scale problem that
317   Fourier neural operator (Kovachki et al., 2023) tried to solve, our framework can be considered as a
318   kind of operator learning.
319   Meta-learning methods (Finn et al., 2017; Rusu et al., 2018; Li et al., 2017; Zintgraf et al., 2019;
320   Perez et al., 2018) aim to learn meta-parameters that can be used across multiple tasks, where the
321   meta parameters are generally learned to make rapid adaptations. In previous meta-learning studies
322   on dynamical systems (Kirchmeyer et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022; Yin et al., 2021a), the objective
323   was to find a meta-function that could quickly adapt to multiple new systems, where hypernetworks
      are only employed as low-rank adaptors for new dynamical system trajectories, similar to the idea


                                                          6
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


324
      of LoRA (Hu et al., 2021). Our work differs from such meta-learning approaches in two key ways.
325   First, we focus on discovering invariant functions rather than quickly adaptable ones. Second, while
326   meta-learning methods seek to learn a single meta-function, our framework learns multiple functions,
327   represented by an invariant function random variable fc . This distinction stems from our more
328   complex environment definition, detailed in Sec. 2.2. From another aspect, learning an invariant
329   function distribution instead of a single function can be considered as generalized meta-learning with
330   an invariant function learning goal.
331   Current invariant learning methods (Arjovsky et al., 2019; Lu et al., 2021a; Rosenfeld et al., 2020;
332   Krueger et al., 2021; Sagawa et al., 2019) follow the framework of invariant risk minimization
333   (IRM) (Arjovsky et al., 2019), which was inspired by invariant causal predictor (Peters et al., 2016).
334   This invariant learning framework aims to learn a hidden invariant representation that generalizes
335   across multiple environments, ensuring out-of-distribution performance. However, this approach
336   cannot work on dynamical forecast tasks due to the lack of invariant function definition and the
337   violation of the categorical data assumption. To be more specific, first, invariant functions cannot
338   be naturally defined in the real number vector space. Second, invariant learning commonly assumes
339
      the prediction results are categorical, where a single invariant representation can fully determine the
      corresponding label. However, this assumption is violated in dynamical system forecasting, where
340
      the invariant mechanism is only partially responsible for the output. In this case, the IRM principle
341
      can not hold even when the invariant function ground truth is provided. To address the issues, we
342   introduce the causal assumption (see Fig. 2) that defines the invariant function space, and propose the
343   corresponding invariant function learning principle and implementation.
344
345
      A related line of research involves symbolic regression for ordinary differential equations (ODEs),
      where transformer models have shown significant success (Becker et al., 2023; d’Ascoli et al., 2023;
346
      Seifner et al., 2024). While these approaches primarily focus on deriving symbolic expressions
347
      for individual trajectories, rather than identifying invariant functions across groups of trajectories,
348   exploring the interplay between these two directions presents an exciting avenue for future research.
349
350   5     E XPERIMENTS
351
352   We conduct experiments to address the following research questions. RQ1: Are existing meta-
353   learning and invariant learning techniques effective for extracting invariant functions? RQ2: Can the
354   proposed invariant function learning principle outperform baseline techniques? RQ3: How do the
355
      full functions f and the invariant functions fc differ in performance? RQ4: Are the extracted invariant
      functions explainable and aligned with the true invariant mechanisms? RQ5: How will performance
356
      change given different lengths of inputs and types of environments? (See Appx. E) RQ6: Is the
357
      proposed hypernetwork implementation more efficient than previous implementations? (See Appx. F)
358
359
360
      5.1   DATASETS
361
362   In our experiments, we introduce three multi-environment (ME) datasets, ME-Pendulum, ME-Lotka-
363   Volterra, and ME-SIREpidemic. These three datasets are generated by simulators modified from the
364   DampedPendulum (Yin et al., 2021b), Lotka-Volterra (Ahmad, 1993), and SIREpidemic (Wang et al.,
365   2021). Specifically, each of the dataset’s training sets includes four environments with 200 samples
366   for each environment. Specifically, each environment corresponds to one specific environmental
367
      effect. ME-Pendulum contains three types of friction and one effect with external energy. ME-Lotka-
      Volterra modified the common predatory relationship into four modified relationships, e.g., adding
368
      resource limits. ME-SIREpidemic produces four conceptual epidemiology models with the same
369
      susceptible population to infected population relationship. In addition to the training set, we generate
370   200 samples with 50 samples for each environment as an in-distribution test set. Please refer to
371   Appx. C for more details.
372
373   5.2   E XPERIMENTAL S ETUP
374
375   To quantitatively evaluate the invariant function extraction performance, we need to remove the
376   environment-related effects to generate invariant trajectories X c as the invariant function ground-
377   truth, e.g., we simulate new data by eliminating −ρωt from −α2 sin(θt ) − ρωt in the ME-Pendulum
      dataset (Fig. 1). To be more specific, a generated invariant trajectory X c , aligning the causal graph, has


                                                           7
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


378
      the same system parameters as the corresponding biased trajectory X for the invariant part controlled
379   by c, i.e., they have the same α in the pendulum example. This invariant trajectory generation is
380   being done on the in-distribution test set so that each trajectory X in this test set has its special
381   corresponding invariant trajectory ground truth X c .
382
      This test set design enables us to mimic the situation of scientific discoveries, where we only
383
      observe environment-biased data but are required to find and evaluate invariant function candidates.
384
      Specifically, given the biased Xp , the hypernetwork is supposed to predict the corresponding invariant
385   derivative function fˆc . Then, with a numeral integrator, the output of this invariant forecaster X̂ c
386   will be evaluated by comparing with the corresponding invariant trajectory ground truth X c using
387   normalized root mean square error (NRMSE).
388
389   5.3   P ROPOSED M ETA - LEARNING AND I NVARIANT L EARNING BASELINES
390
391   General dynamical system forecasting is different from invariant function learning significantly,
392   where they focus on how to adapt to new trajectories, which commonly requires further optimization,
393   e.g., test-time adaptation (Mouli et al., 2024) or adaptations with meta information (Wang et al.,
394   2022; Kirchmeyer et al., 2022). Unfortunately, under our scientific discovery setting, there is no extra
395   information provided at test time, making them inapplicable to this setting. Therefore, we construct 4
396
      new baseline settings by transplanting the techniques of previous meta-learning and invariant learning
      to our proposed framework detailed in Appx. D.1.
397
398   We first adopt the meta-learning baseline MAML (Finn et al., 2017), where we use its learned meta-
399   parameters for invariant learning to evaluate whether the fastest adapted parameter is the invariant
400   function parameter. Our second meta-learning baseline is CoDA (Kirchmeyer et al., 2022), where we
401   replace its hypernetwork decoder with the full encoder-decoder hypernetwork in our framework to fit
402
      in our task. Aligning with the original CoDA paper, we set the dimension of the hidden representation
      to be 2. Similar to MAML, we eliminate the adaptation part and use only the learned meta-parameter
403
      for invariant state prediction.
404
405   For invariant learning baselines, we adopted the two most typical techniques, IRM (Ahuja et al.,
406   2021) and VREx (Krueger et al., 2021). These two techniques are applied to the proposed framework,
407   where IRM stands for the most typical definition of invariant learning, while VREx stands for
408
      the distributionally robust optimization baseline, which can be considered as the generalization of
      GroupDRO (Sagawa et al., 2019).
409
410
411   5.4   Q UANTITATIVE RESULTS
412
      Similar to other scientific discovery tasks, such as drug discovery, constructing a proper validation
413
      set is challenging. Instead, with only observational data available, we generate invariant function
414   candidates that can be further validated in real experimental settings, e.g., through the introduction of
415   interventions (Pearl, 2009).
416
417
      To quantitatively compare these methods, we provide the corresponding hyper-parameter search
      spaces for each technique in Appx. D and plot the results of random hyper-parameter sampling as
418
      distributions using Boxen plots. As shown in Fig. 4, we compare the quality of the invariant function
419
      candidates based on their median, best result, and quantiles. Specifically, the median performance
420   of our proposed method surpasses the middle candidates of all other approaches. The performance
421   gaps are particularly notable on the ME-Pendulum and ME-SIR-Epidemic datasets. For example,
422   on ME-Pendulum, over 75% of our method’s candidates outperform the best results of MAML and
423   CoDA, and more than 93.75% candidates of IRM and VREx. On ME-Lotka-Volterra, the median of
424   our candidates still outperforms nearly all candidates from other methods. In addition, as shown in
425   the visualizations on ME-Pendulum 5, our learned invariant function fˆc eliminates environmental
426   resistances from the original trajectory (Fig. 5a) and obtain a simple pendulum motion without
427   attenuation (Fig. 5b). Both quantitative and visualization results demonstrate the superior capability
428
      of our method in extracting invariant functions (RQ2).
429   To address the first research question (RQ1), we observe that the invariant learning techniques, IRM
430   and VREx, are generally more stable than the meta-learning baselines. Although IRM and VREx do
431   not surpass MAML on ME-Lotka-Volterra, they outperform MAML on 2 out of 3 datasets and are
      consistently better than CoDA. However, when compared to our proposed method, the best function

478   invariant learning principles fall short in the context of invariant function extraction, aligning with the
479   discussions in Sec. 4.
480
481   5.5       F ULL F UNCTION V. S . I NVARIANT F UNCTION
482
483   To analyze RQ3, we benchmark our best invariant function learning models on the invariant state
484   ground truth Xc and the multi-environment state ground truth X, comparing their results using the
485   predicted invariant function f̂c and the full function f̂. As shown in Tab. 2, the performance on Xc using
      f̂c represents the core results of our invariant function learning approach. In contrast, the predictions


                                                                           9
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


486
      on Xc using f̂ serve as a baseline for the ablation study, where no invariant function learning principle
487
      is applied. Additionally, the prediction error on X using f̂c implies the environmental information
488
      eliminated by the invariant function learning principle, while the NRMSEs on X using f̂ reflect
489
      standard in-distribution (ID) test errors. We observe that the ME-Lotka-Volterra dataset is the most
490   challenging, with an NRMSE of 0.3881 in the ID test. This result is consistent with general deep
491   learning outcomes in Mouli et al. (2024), given that ME-Lotka-Volterra is more complex than its
492   original version.
493
494
      As expected, f̂ performs well on X, while f̂c excels in predicting Xc . In our ablation study, we
      compare the performance of invariant state predictions Xc across all datasets. The predicted invariant
495
      functions f̂c significantly outperform the full predicted functions f̂ in terms of NRMSE, validating the
496
      effectiveness of the proposed invariant function learning principle.Furthermore, we conduct more
497
      strict ablation study with independent f̂ and f̂c training in Appx. E.1.
498
499
500
      5.6    S YMBOLIC R EGRESSION E XPLANATION
501   Furthermore, to address RQ4, we analyze the extracted invariant functions f̂c by applying symbolic
502   regression using PySR (Cranmer, 2023). As shown in Tab. 2, we compare the symbolic regression
503   (SR) explanations of the extracted invariant functions f̂c with the true invariant functions fc . On
504   the ME-Pendulum dataset, the frictionless pendulum function is nearly perfectly extracted, with
505   0.99ωt matching ωt and −0.97α2 sin (θt ) closely approximating the true −α2 sin (θt ). Given the
506   complexity of the ME-Lotka-Volterra dataset, the extracted invariant functions f̂c are non-trivial
507   and significantly outperform the full function f̂. On the ME-SIREpidemic dataset, the near-perfect
508   NRMSE for the invariant state indicates that the invariant function must have been correctly extracted.
509   However, although the expression for dR    dt
                                                   t
                                                     is correct, the extracted expressions for dS
                                                                                                dt
                                                                                                  t
                                                                                                    and dI
                                                                                                        dt
                                                                                                          t
                                                                                                            do
                                                                                dSt
510   not precisely match the expected fc . Specifically, the coefficient from dt does not equal the inverse
511   of the coefficient from dIt
                               dt
                                  , though this discrepancy should be constant. These mismatches attribute
512   to the limitations of PySR given the large number of variables and samples.1 Future work could
513   explore incorporating stronger inductive biases, similar to physics-informed machine learning (PIML)
514   methods (Mouli et al., 2024; Yin et al., 2021b; Cranmer et al., 2020), to address these challenges.
515   Please refer to Appx. E.3 for symbolic comparisons with all baseline.
516
517   6     L IMITATIONS
518
519
      While this work provides a foundation for invariant function learning in dynamical systems, several
      limitations and opportunities for future exploration remain. These include extending the framework
520
      to more complex entanglements, exploring applications in PDE systems, strengthening theoretical
521
      guarantees with advanced error bounds, and developing comprehensive benchmarks. Additionally,
522   broader applications such as generalizable physics learning and foundational model development
523   represent exciting directions for further research. Please refer to Appx. I for more discussions.
524
525
      7     C ONCLUSION
526
527   In this work, we target addressing the challenge of the invariant mechanism discovery in ODE
528   dynamical systems by extending invariant learning into function spaces. We introduce a new task,
529   invariant function learning, which aims to extract the invariant dynamics across all environments with
530   different environment-specific function forms. We design a causal analysis based disentanglement
531   framework DIF to expose the underlying invariant functions. Additionally, we propose an invari-
532   ant function learning principle with theoretical guarantees to optimize the framework and ensure
533
      effective invariant function discovery. Our experiments, including invariant trajectory validations,
      visualizations, ablation studies, and symbolic regression analyses, demonstrate the effectiveness of
534
      our method. Finally, as discussed in Sec. 6, the introduced invariant function learning task has wide
535
      application scenarios and many challenges remain to be addressed. We expect that our work will
536   shed light on numerous future explorations in this field.
537
538
          1
539         Superior PySR explanations indicate great invariant function learning results, but effective invariant function
      learning results might not lead to good PySR explanations.

543   are included in Appx. B. For the datasets, the system parameters, simulator expressions, and datasets
544   visualizations are provided in Appx. C. For the model, we provide full details including all the training
545   setup, architecture, objectives, and hyper-parameter searching spaces in Appx. D. The finalized code
546
      will be released upon acceptance.

             fc                         An invariant derivative function
             fe                         An environment derivative function

             H(X)                       Shannon entropy of the matrix-valued random variable X
             EX∼P [f (X)] or Ef (X)     Expectation of f (X) with respect to p(X)

             X∼P                        Random variable X has distribution P

             X ∼ p(X)                   X is sampled from distribution p(X)

      B     I NVARIANT FUNCTION LEARNING FOUNDATION
855
856
      B.1   S TRUCTURAL CAUSAL MODEL
857
858   In this section, we discuss the trajectory generation process under the Structural Causal Model (SCM)
859   assumption in Fig. 6. To begin with, this SCM is a directed acyclic graph (DAG) with the following
860   components:
861
862          • Exogenous variables U = {c, e, X0 , ϵc , ϵe , ϵp , ϵ} are not caused by any variables within the
863            model and are from their own independent distributions. Here ϵc , ϵe , ϵp , ϵ are noise terms
               introduced during the function generation and trajectory integral.

      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    Function generation                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            Trajectory generation
