875
876
877
             • Endogenous variables V = {fc , fe , f, Xc , Xp , X} are caused by the causal mappings within
               the model.
878
879          • Structural equations define the direct causation in the model.
880               – fc := gc (c, ϵc )
881               – fe := ge (e, ϵe )
882               – f := gcomp (fc , fe )
883               – Xc := gint (fc , X0 ) + ϵ
884                          Tc
                  – Xp := gint   (f, X0 ) + ϵp
885
                  – X := gint (f, X0 ) + ϵ
886                                                        Tc
887
               where gint is a T -step integrator, while gint only applies integral for Tc steps. While gc , ge ,
               gcomp are assumed to be unknown, gint is assumed to be an ideal integrator with an extra
888
               random noise ϵ to model the real world situations. Note that the cause of X can be written
889
               as fc and fe without side effects, so f is used for analytical purposes. Therefore, gcomp is a
890            conceptual function composition without introducing noises.
891
892
893   B.2    P ROOF OF I NVARIANT FUNCTION LEARNING PRINCIPLE
894
      Theorem B.1 (Invariant Function Learning Principle 3.1). Given the causal graph in Fig. 2 and the
895
      predicted function random variable f̂c = hθc (Xp ), the true invariant function random variable is
896
      fc = hθc∗ (Xp ), where θc∗ is the solution to the following optimization problem:
897
898                         θc∗ = arg max I(hθc (Xp ); f|X0 )                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   subject to hθc (Xp ) ⊥⊥ e,                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       (4)
                                      θc
899
900   where I(·; ·) denotes mutual information, which quantifies the informational overlap between the
901   predicted invariant function hθc (Xp ) and the true full-dynamics function f.
902
903   Proof. Existence:
904   We first prove the existence of a solution θc∗ to the optimization problem, such that fc = hθc∗ (Xp ). To
905   establish this, we proceed by contradiction. Assume no such θc∗ exists, implying that I(fc ; f|X0 ) is not
906   maximized. Then, there must exist some f′c such that f′c ⊥⊥ e and I(f′c ; f|X0 ) > I(fc ; f|X0 ). Given the
907   mutual information expression I(fc ; f|X0 ) = H(f|X0 ) − H(f|fc , X0 ), this inequality implies:
908                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             H(f|fc ) > H(f|f′c ).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            (5)
909
910   Since X0 is independent of f and fc (as per Fig. 2), and given that f = gf (fc , fe ) implies H(f|fc , fe ) = 0,
911   we derive:
912                                              H(f|fc ) = H(fe ).                                                (6)
913
914   Similarly, for f′c , we have:
915
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               H(f|f′c ) ≥ H(fe |f′c ).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          (7)
916
      Combining these results with Eq. 5, we obtain:
917
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 H(fe ) > H(fe |f′c ),                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           (8)


                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   17
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


918
      which contradicts the independence condition f′c ⊥⊥ e, as this would require H(fe ) = H(fe |f′c ).
919   Therefore, a solution θc∗ exists, satisfying fc = hθc∗ (Xp ).
920
921   Uniqueness: We now prove that for any solution θc∗ of the optimization process, it holds that
922
      fc = hθc∗ (Xp ).
923   We use a proof by contradiction. Assume that there exists another solution f′c ̸= fc that satisfies the
924   independence constraint and achieves the maximum mutual information. By assumption, we have
925   H(e) = H(e|f′c ) and I(f′c ; f|X0 ) = I(fc ; f|X0 ), which implies H(f|fc ) = H(f|f′c ).
926   Since f = gf (fc , fe ), we expand the entropy terms:
927
928                                          H(f|fc ) = H(fc , fe |fc ) = H(fe ),                            (9)
929
      and
930
                         H(f|f′c ) = H(fc , fe |f′c ) = H(fc |f′c ) + H(fe |f′c ) = H(fc |f′c ) + H(fe ).   (10)
931
932   Substituting H(f|fc ) = H(f|f′c ) into these equations, we find:
933
934                                                      H(fc |f′c ) = 0.                                   (11)
935
936   Based on this, we now aim to prove that fc = f′c . First, given H(f′c |fc ) ≥ 0, we need to show that
937   H(f′c |fc ) = 0. Assume that H(f′c |fc ) > 0. In this case, f′c can determine fc while containing more
938
      information than fc , all while remaining independent of fe . This would imply that H(f′ |f) > 0, where
      f′ = gf (f′ , fe ), which would in turn affect the corresponding prediction, leading to H(X′ |X) > 0. Such
939
      a situation would violate the MSE minimization condition.
940
941   Therefore, we must have both H(f′c |fc ) = 0 and H(fc |f′c ) = 0. This implies that fc and f′c are
942   isomorphic functions, i.e., there exists a bijective function gb such that fc = gb (f′c ).
943   Furthermore, since minimizing the MSE of X is equivalent to minimizing the MSE of dX           given X0 ,
                                                                                                  dt
944   this minimization ensures that, for the same fe , fc (X) = f′c (X) for all X . As a result, the bijective
945   function gb must be the identity mapping, and we conclude that f′c = fc in the support of p(X).
946
      Thus, for any solution θc∗ of the optimization process, it follows that fc = hθc∗ (Xp ).
947
948
949
950
951   B.3    P ROOF OF ODE CROSS - ENTROPY MINIMIZATION
952
      Lemma B.2 (ODE cross-entropy minimization 3.2). Given our forecasting model p(X|hθ (Xp ), X0 ), it
953
      follows that the cross-entropy minimization between the data distribution p(X) and p(X|hθ (Xp ), X0 )
954
      is equivalent to minimizing mean square error minθ EX∼p ∥X − X̂∥22 , where X̂ is sampled from
955   p(X|hθ (Xp ), X0 ).
956
957
      Proof. The forecasting optimization goal is to use our framework to approximate the data dis-
958   tribution p(X), parameterized as p(X|hθ (Xp ), X0 ), where we apply the cross-entropy minimiza-
959   tion, i.e.,H(p(X), p(X|hθ (Xp ), X0 )) = −EX∼p [log p(X|hθ (Xp ), X0 )]. Furthermore, this negative
960   log-likelihood optimization can be further reduced to the common mean squared error (MSE).
961
962
                                                   min EX∼p ∥X − X̂∥22 ,                                    (12)
963                                                  θ
964
965   where X̂ ∼ p(X|hθ (Xp ), X0 ). Since the distribution p(X|hθ (Xp ), X0 ) is modeled    as a Gaussian
966   N X; gint (hθ (Xp ), X0 ), σ 2 I (Sec. 3.1), we have X̂ = µ + ϵ, where ϵ ∼ N 0, σ 2 I .
967
                                       min −EX∼p [log p(X|hθ (Xp ), X0 )]
968                                     θ

969                                               n                 1
                                     = min EX∼p     log(2πσ 2 ) + 2 ∥X − µ∥22                               (13)
970                                     θ         2                2σ

971                                                1                  n
                                     = min EX∼p        ∥X − µ∥22 + log(2πσ 2 )
                                        θ         2σ 2                2


                                                               18
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


972
       Since n2 log(2πσ 2 ) is a constant, we ignore it in the minimization process.
973
974

                                    1           2        n
975                     min EX∼p        ∥X − µ∥ 2 +         log(2πσ 2 )
                         θ         2σ 2                  2
976
                                    1

                                                          2
977                   = min EX∼p        ∥X − (X̂  −  ϵ)∥  2
                         θ         2σ 2
978
                                    1                  2
979                   = min EX∼p        ∥X − X̂ +   ϵ∥ 2                                                  (14)
                         θ         2σ 2
980
                                    1             2           2              T
981                   = min EX∼p        (∥X − X̂∥ 2  +   ∥ϵ∥  2 + 2(X  − X̂)   ϵ)
                         θ         2σ 2
982
                                    1                       1              1
983                   = min EX∼p        ∥X − X̂∥22 + 2 Eϵ [∥ϵ∥22 ] + 2 EX∼p (X − X̂)T Eϵ [ϵ]
984                      θ         2σ 2                   2σ               σ
985                                                h                 i
986    Here, 2σ1 2 EX∼p [∥ϵ∥22 ] is a constant; EX∼p σ12 (X − X̂)T ϵ = σ12 EX∼p (X − X̂)T Eϵ [ϵ] = 0 since ϵ is
987    independently sampled with a zero mean. Therefore,
988
                                    1            2    1                 1
989                     min EX∼p        ∥X − X̂∥ 2 +      Eϵ [∥ϵ∥22 ] + 2 EX∼p (X − X̂)T Eϵ [ϵ]
                         θ         2σ 2              2σ 2              σ
990
991                                 1            2                                                        (15)
                      = min EX∼p        ∥X − X̂∥ 2
992                      θ         2σ 2
                                 h           i
993                   = min EX∼p ∥X − X̂∥22
                         θ
994
995
996    This reduction connects the information theory and the practical MSE optimization, which further
997
       helps us to transform the mutual information maximization into a similar MSE optimization below.
998
999
1000
1001
       B.4   P ROOF OF ODE CONDITIONAL MUTUAL INFORMATION MAXIMIZATION
1002
1003   Proposition B.3 (Proof of ODE conditional mutual information maximization 3.3). Given fore-
1004   casting model p(X|hθc (Xp ), X0 ), it follows that the conditional mutual information maximization
1005   maxθc I(hθc (Xp ); f|X0 ) is equivalent to minimizing mean square error minθc EX∼P ∥X − X̂ c ∥22 , where
1006   X̂ c is the predicted trajectory sampled from p(X|hθc (Xp ), X0 ).
1007
1008
1009   Proof. According to Lemma 3.2, we can reduce a negative log-likelihood minimization
1010   minθc −EX∼p log p(X|hθc (Xp ), X0 ) to minθc EX∼P ∥X − X̂ c ∥22 .
1011   Therefore, we only need to prove the equivalence between the −EX∼p log p(X|hθc (Xp ), X0 ) mini-
1012   mization and the I(hθc (Xp ); f|X0 ) maximization. It follows that
1013
1014                                      max I(hθc (Xp ); f|X0 )
                                           θc
1015                                                                                                      (16)
                                        = max H(f|X0 ) − H(f|hθc (Xp ), X0 ).
1016                                       θc
1017
1018   Since H(f|X0 ) is a constant, we ignore it in the maximization process and obtain
1019
                                          max H(f|X0 ) − H(f|hθc (Xp ), X0 )
1020                                        θc

1021                                    = max −H(f|hθc (Xp ), X0 )                                        (17)
                                            θc
1022
1023                                    = min H(f|hθc (Xp ), X0 ).
                                           θc
1024
1025   Since X = gint (f, X0 ) + ϵ where ϵ is an indepedent random noise, H(X|hθc (Xp ), X0 , f) =
       H(ϵ|hθc (Xp )) = H(ϵ). Since given specific θc , H(hθc (Xp )|X) = 0, with the conditional entropy


                                                         19
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1026
       chain rule, it follows that
1027
                                     min H(f|hθc (Xp ), X0 )
1028                                  θc

1029                             = min H(f, X|hθc (Xp ), X0 ) − H(X|hθc (Xp ), X0 , f)
                                      θc
1030
1031                             = min H(f, X|hθc (Xp ), X0 ) − H(ϵ)
                                      θc
1032
                                 = min H(f, X|hθc (Xp ), X0 )
1033                                  θc

1034                             = min H(f|hθc (Xp ), X0 , X) + H(X|hθc (Xp ), X0 )                            (18)
                                      θc
1035
                                 = min H(f|X0 , X) + H(X|hθc (Xp ), X0 )
1036                                  θc
1037                             = min H(X|hθc (Xp ), X0 )
                                      θc
1038
1039                             = min −EX,Xp ,X0 ∼p log p(X|hθc (Xp ), X0 )
                                      θc
1040
                                 = min −EX∼p log p(X|hθc (Xp ), X0 )
1041                                  θc

1042   where H(f|X0 , X) is a constant. Here, we finish building the equivalence between the function
1043   conditional mutual information maximization and the trajectory negative log-likelihood minimization.
1044   Using the proof in Lemma 3.2, our final optimization goal can be reduced to
1045                                        min EX∼P ∥X − X̂ c ∥22 .                                  (19)
                                                      θc
1046
1047
1048
       B.5    T HEORETICAL J USTIFICATION FOR A DVERSARIAL T RAINING
1049
1050   To incorporate the independence constraint, we enforce the condition f̂c ⊥⊥ e, where f̂c = hθc (Xp ) is
1051   the predicted function random variable, not a realization. Since f̂c ⊥⊥ e is equivalent to I(e; f̂c ) = 0,
1052
       and I(e; f̂c ) ≥ 0, the objective I(e; f̂c ) reaches its minimum when and only when f̂c ⊥⊥ e. This leads
1053
       us to define minimizing I(e; f̂c ) as our training criterion.
1054
       Definition 1. The environment independence training criterion is
1055
1056                                                 θc∗ = arg min I(e; f̂c ).                                 (20)
                                                               θc
1057
                                                                                                      h              i
1058   This training criterion can be used directly, since the mutual information I(e; f̂c ) = E log PP(e|(e)f̂c )
1059
1060
       while P (e|f̂c ) is unknown. Following Proposition 1 in GAN Goodfellow et al. (2020), we introduce
1061   an optimal discriminator gϕ : F 7→ E with parameters  h ϕ to approximate
                                                                             i        the unknown P (e|f̂c ) as
1062   Pϕ (e|f̂c ), minimizing the negative log-likelihood −E log Pϕ (e|f̂c ) . We then have the following two
1063   propositions:
1064   Proposition B.4. For θc fixed, the optimal discriminator ϕ is
1065
                                                              h               i
                                               ϕ∗ = arg min −E log Pϕ (e|f̂c ) .                               (21)
1066                                                       ϕ
1067
1068   This proposition can be proved straightforwardly by applying the cross-entropy training criterion.
1069   Proposition B.5. Denoting KL-divergence as KL[·∥·], for θc fixed, the optimal discriminator ϕ is ϕ∗ ,
1070   such that                              h                      i
1071
                                           KL P (e|f̂c )∥Pϕ∗ (e|f̂c ) = 0.                             (22)
1072
1073   Proof. Given a fixed θc , both I(e; f̂c ) and H(e) are constants. Therefore, we have:
1074
1075
                                                    h               i
                                     ϕ∗ = arg min −E log Pϕ (e|f̂c )
1076                                             ϕ
                                                                   h               i
1077
                                           = arg min I(e; f̂c ) − E log Pϕ (e|f̂c ) − H(e)                     (23)
1078                                             ϕ
                                                           h                  i
1079
                                           = arg min KL P (e|f̂c )∥Pϕ (e|f̂c ) .
                                                 ϕ



                                                                20
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1080
       Thus, minimizing the negative log-likelihood of Pϕ (e|f̂c ) is equivalent to minimizing the KL diver-
1081
       gence between   P (e|f̂c ) andiits approximation Pϕ (e|f̂c ). Since KL divergence is bounded by 0, we
1082           h
       have KL P (e|f̂c )∥Pϕ∗ (e|f̂c ) = 0. This concludes the proof.
1083
1084
1085   With these propositions, the mutual information can be computed with the help of the optimal
1086   discriminator ϕ∗ . According to Proposition B.5, we have:
                                         h                i            h                       i
1087                       I(e; f̂c ) = E log Pϕ∗ (e|f̂c ) + H(e) + KL P (e|f̂c )∥Pϕ∗ (e|f̂c )
1088                                     h                i                                               (24)
1089                                  = E log Pϕ∗ (e|f̂c ) + H(e) + 0.
1090
1091   Thus, by disregarding the constant H(e), the training criterion becomes:
1092
                                       θc∗ = arg min I(e; f̂c )
1093                                             θc
1094                                                  h                i
                                           = arg min E log Pϕ∗ (e|f̂c )                                   (25)
1095                                             θc
1096                                                               h          i
1097                                       = arg min max E log Pϕ (e|f̂c )         ,
                                                 θc       ϕ
1098
1099
       where Pϕ (e|f̂c ) is the probability modeling of gϕ . Therefore, the log-likelihood adversarial training
1100
       can enforce the independence hθc (Xp ) ⊥⊥ e.
1101
1102
1103   C     DATASETS
1104
1105   C.1   BASIC SETUP
1106
       We conduct experiments on the proposed three multi-environment datasets ME-Pendulum, ME-Lotka-
1107
       Volterra, and ME-SIREpidemic. Each of these datasets includes 1000 samples, where 800 and 200
1108   samples are assigned to training set and test set, respectively. Each training set has 4 environments
1109   where 200 samples are generated in each environment. Each sample is observed over 10 units of time,
1110   and each time is discretized by regularly-spaced discrete time steps from t0 to tT , where T = 99, i.e.,
1111   there are 100 time intervals of 0.1 unit of time each. A sample generation process is controlled by a
1112   set of ODEs with common parameters Wc ∼ U(Wlow       c     c
                                                               , Whigh ) and environment-specific parameters
1113   We ∼ U(Wlow  e     e
                       , Whigh ) sampled from their uniform distributions, e.g., in the pendulum system,
1114   Wc = {α} and We = {ρ}, where α ∼ U(αlow , αhigh ) and ρ ∼ U(ρlow , ρhigh ). Note that each
1115   environment has its specific function form with its environment-specific parameters We .
1116   The environment split is the same in the test set. The only difference is that in the test set each
1117   sample X has one additional prediction target X c with only the invariant dynamics. For example, the
1118   invariant trajectory X c of the one generated by −α2 sin θt − ρωt will be created by −α2 sin θt .
1119
1120   C.2   ME-P ENDULUM
1121
1122   ME-Pendulum is motivated by the DampedPendulum system (Yin et al., 2021b). The state Xt =
1123
       [θt , ωt ] ∈ R2 are the angle and angular velocity of the pendulum at time t, where we have Wc = {α},
       We = {ρ}, and TC = T3 . The underlying invariant ODE is dθ           dt
                                                                              t
                                                                                = ωt , dω
                                                                                        dt
                                                                                          t
                                                                                            = −α2 sin (θt ). As
1124
       shown in Tab. 4, this invariant ODE is entangled with different environmental factors, forming four
1125
       environments, namely, damped, powered, spring, air.
1126
1127
       C.3   ME-L OTKA -VOLTERRA
1128
1129   Motivated by the Lotka-Volterra system (Ahmad, 1993), the state Xt = [pt , qt ] ∈ R2 are the population
1130   of preys and predators at time t, where we have Wc = {α, β, γ, δ}, We = {α′ , β′ , γ ′ , δ ′ }, and
1131   TC = T2 . The underlying invariant ODEs are dp
                                                    dt
                                                       = αp − βpq, dq dt
                                                                         = δpq − γq . As shown in Tab. 5,
1132   these invariant ODEs are entangled in 4 environments, i.e., save, fight, resource, omnivore. The
1133   save environment ODEs simulate the decrease of food wastage along with the increase of predators.
       The fight environment ODEs simulate the decrease of hunting efficiency along with the increase of

1177   C.4    ME-SIRE PIDEMIC
1178
1179   In the SIREpidemic (Wang et al., 2021) system, the states Xt = [St , It , Rt ] ∈ R3 are the suscepti-
1180   ble, infected, and recovered individuals at time t, respectively. In this adapted ME-SIREpidemic
1181   system, we have Wc = {β}, We = {γ}, and Tc = T2 . The underlying invariant ODEs are
       dS            SI
1182    dt
           = −β S+I+R    , dI
                           dt
                                    SI
                              = β S+I+R  , dR
                                           dt
                                              = 0, where we only care about the S to I transformation
1183   relationship. As shown in Tab. 6, we introduce 4 environments, origin, enlarge, loop, negative. These
1184
       four environments describe four different models, where some of them are only for math modeling.
       The origin environment is the same as the original SIREpidemic model. The enlarge environment
1185
       ODEs expand the epidemic range. The loop environment ODEs include deaths and second-time
1186
       infections. The negative environment ODEs is a pure math model allowing negative numbers.

1237   D     E XPERIMENTAL DETAILS
1238
1239   We conduct experiments on 800-sample training sets with a training batch size of 32, which leads
1240   to 25 iterations per epoch. For each run, we optimize the neural network with 2,000 epochs, which
1241   is equivalent to 50,000 iterations. Given fixed learning iterations, the learning rate is selected from
       U(1e − 4, 1e − 3).


                                                               23
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1242
       D.1     BASELINES
1243
1244   In our experiments, we design four adapted baselines, since this new task has never been explored
1245   before. The selection of baselines is based on the following two questions.
1246
1247           • As invariant learning has been successfully applied in many representation learning tasks,
1248             can general invariant learning principle still work for invariant function learning?
1249           • Meta-learning techniques has been well designed to solved problems in dynamical systems
1250             due to their quick adaptation characteristics. However, there is no evidence that the quick
1251             adaptable functions are the invariant function that shares across environments. Are they?
1252
1253   For the first question, since our method is based on enforcing independence which shares a similar
1254   philosophy as domain adversarial neural network Ganin et al. (2016), we consider other invariant
1255   methods going different ways. There are many methods well-known in the field of invariant learning,
1256   but considering our invariant function learning formulation, we found the discovery of invariant
1257   function requires independence that is different from the most typical "well performed across all
1258
       environments" invariant learning requirements. Therefore, as a validation of our guess, we adapt two
       widely used and known invariant learning methods, IRM Arjovsky et al. (2019) and VREx Krueger
1259
       et al. (2021). These two techniques are directly applied to our framework with the same architecture
1260
       and their typical hyper-parameters searching spaces:
1261
1262           • λirm ∼ U (1e − 2, 1e2)
1263
1264           • λvrex ∼ U (1e − 1, 1e3)
1265
1266
       As shown in our result plot 4, the results are not surprising. That means that the function can perform
       well across multiple environments is not the invariant function.
1267
1268   For the second question, our initial guess is that meta-learning is very sensitive to the distribution of
1269   the training set. If certain pattern exists in multiple environments (not all), the meta-learning methods
1270   are prone to capture it as a part of the meta-function, which satisfies their quick adaptation goals.
1271   In our experiments, we choose MAML Finn et al. (2017) and CoDA Kirchmeyer et al. (2022) as
1272
       our adapted baselines. CoDA is adapted since it uses hypernetwork as a full network adaptor which
       is similar to our framework. However, CoDA is applied on coefficient-environments and requires
1273
       test-time adaptation without a trajectory encoder, leading to significant architecture differences.
1274
       Therefore, we apply CoDA as meta-learning techniques focusing on its low-dimension (2-dimension)
1275   environment representation and regularization. MAML is selected as the most typical meta-learning
1276   baseline, which does not require the use of hypernetwork, and only learns a meta function used to
1277   predict invariant trajectories. Their typical hyper-parameters searching spaces are shown as follows.
1278
1279           • λcoda ∼ U (1e − 5, 1e − 3)
1280
               • λmaml ∼ U (1e − 3, 1)        (Meta learning rate)
1281
1282
1283
       D.2     D ISENTANGLEMENT OF I NVARIANT F UNCTION SETUP
1284
       D.2.1    A RCHITECTURE
1285
1286   Transformer. For the trajectory encoder in our hypernetwork, we apply a 6-layer 8-head 256-
1287   dimension FFN transformer Vaswani (2017) with frequency positional encoding Gehring et al. (2017).
1288   We tried different architectures like GRUs Cho (2014), but the transformer encoder can provide the
1289   best in-domain test performance easily. We also sweeped to the depth, width, and number of heads,
1290   and found that 6-layer 8-head 256-dimension FFN transformer is strong enough for our ODE systems
1291   without making training difficult.
1292   Function embedding. In the hidden function embedding space, we select the function embedding
1293   dimension to be 32 or 64, while these two selections perform quite similar. For the MLPs used to
1294   disentangle and decode hidden function embedding, we use 3-layer MLPs with ReLU as the activa-
1295   tions. While for the decoder, the last layer projects the hidden function embedding to parameterized
       function space Rm where m is the number of parameters in the derivative neural network.


                                                           24
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1296
       Derivative function network. The derivative neural network is a 4-layer or 5-layer MLP with width
1297   16 or 32, which takes Xt ∈ Rd as input and output dX t
                                                              ∈ Rd . This neural network is transformed to
                                                          dt
1298   be a functional in our implementation.
1299
1300
       Discriminator. Our discriminator is a 3 to 6 layer MLP with width 64 or 128. The size of discrimina-
       tor can be easily chosen since the main goal of it is to discriminate the environment information in
1301
       the hidden function space. Therefore, the simplest way to filter non-qualified discriminators is using
1302
       the prediction entropy of Pϕ (e|f̂e ), since f̂e should contain rich environment information, different to
1303
       the prediction from f̂c . Our experiments also validate that the failure to distinguish f̂e will always
1304
       cause the failure of invariant function discovery, which is natural, since the adversarial training is
1305
       based on the optimal discriminators Goodfellow et al. (2020).
1306
1307   Note that for all our MLPs, we apply one LayerNorm before each activation.2
1308
1309   D.2.2       T RAINING OBJECTIVES
1310
1311
1312   We restate our three additional strategies here. Firstly, the adversarial training of f̂c will cause the
1313   loss of environment information in f̂c , leading to the training difficulty of the discriminator; therefore,
1314   this discriminatorh is not onlyitrained on f̂c but also on f̂e with the same hyper-parameter λdis , i.e.,
1315
       minϕ,θ −EX,e∼P log Pϕ (e|fˆe ) . Secondly, instead of using the large fˆc and fˆe as the input of the
1316
       discriminator, we input the the corresponding embeddings zc and ze . Thirdly, to avoid the use of a
1317
       numerical or neural integrator which causes long training time, we follow Mouli et al. (2024) to fit
1318
       derivatives only. That is, instead of using the inference forecaster p(X|hθc (Xp ), X0 ), we calculate the
1319   derivatives of X using fˆ and fˆc , and replace the MSE over trajectory matrices with the MSE over
1320   derivative matrices. Note that this modification only eliminates the use of integrator for stability
1321   during training and thus does not affect our analysis and optimization goal.
1322
       We introduce our hyper-parameter searching space as follows.
1323
1324
1325
                 • λc ∼ U(1e − 7, 1e − 4)
1326             • λdis ∼ U (1e − 1, 1)
1327
1328             • λ′adv ∼ U (1e2, 1e6)
1329             • λadv = λc · λ′adv
1330
1331
       The most critical hyper-parameters are λc and λadv which control the information overlap between
1332   fc and f. Conceptually, λc controls the conditional mutual information (MI) maximization in our
1333   invariant function learning principle, while λadv enforces the independence constraint. The intensity
1334   of the independence enforcing λadv is dependent on the intensity of MI maximization λc ; thus, we set
1335   λadv according to λc , leading to λadv = λc · λ′adv .
1336
       λdis is only discriminator training, which is relatively trivial according to our discriminator descrip-
1337
       tions in Appx. D.2.1.
1338
1339
1340   D.2.3       M ETRIC
1341
       Root mean square deviation (RMSE) is a commonly used metric, but it suffers difficulties when
1342
       comparing datasets with different value scales. Therefore, we normalize it using its standard deviation.
1343
1344
                                                           q
1345
                                                            EX∼p ∥X − X̂∥22
1346                                          NRMSE =                                                         (26)
                                                                 Std(X)

          2
              The code will be released upon acceptance.

       D.3    S OFTWARE AND H ARDWARE
1378
1379   Our implementation is under the architecture of PyTorch Paszke et al. (2019). The deployment
1380   environments are Ubuntu 20.04 with 48 Intel(R) Xeon(R) Silver, 4214R CPU @ 2.40GHz, 755GB
1381   RAM, and graphics cards NVIDIA RTX 2080Ti.
1382
1383
       E     S UPPLEMENTARY EXPERIMENTS
1384
1385
       E.1   A BLATION STUDY
1386
1387   As a complementary ablation study of Sec. 5.5, we train two models by eliminating two important
1388   components from the original model, namely, f pipeline and fc pipeline. The f pipeline removes
1389   the discriminator and only output fˆ to the forecasting, which neglects the disentanglement process.
1390   The fc pipeline prunes the fˆ output while maintaining the adversarial training process. As shown
1391   in Fig. 10, both f and fc pipelines fail to perform valid invariant function learning aligning with
1392
       our theoretical results. Specifically, the f pipeline faces difficulties in extracting invariant functions
       without environment information. The unsatisfactory performance of the fc pipeline is attributed
1393
       to the discriminator’s training failure. This is because the training of the discriminator requires the
1394
       capture of environment information, but the elimination of the fˆ part also removes the training of ẑe ,
1395   the critical environment information captor. Therefore, the discriminator loses the most important
1396   environment information input, leading to training failure.
1397
1398
       E.2   I NPUT LENGTH AND ENVIRONMENT ANALYSIS

1400   In order to ensure fairness, we fix the input length and the number of environments in our experiments.
1401   However, it is also interesting to figure out the effects of the input trajectory length Tc and the
1402   number of environments on the model performance. Fig. 11 shows the performance of DIF given
1403   different input length factor lt , where Tc = lTt . The results indicate the input length does not affect
       the performance significantly, where the only variances are attributed to the training difficulty of the

1442   transformer given different input lengths. Therefore, a shorter input length can perform slightly better
1443   given the same training steps.
1444
       For the environment analysis, in addition to evaluations on the full set of environments, we benchmark
1445
       model performance on datasets with three and two training environments. Specifically, we select
1446   [Powered, Air, Spring] and [Powered, Air] for ME-Pendulum; [Save, Fight, Resource] and [Save,
1447   Fight] for ME-Lotka-Volterra; and [Negative, Origin, Enlarge] and [Negative, Origin] for ME-
1448   SIREpidemic. While not all possible environment combinations are evaluated, these selections
1449   provide intriguing insights. As illustrated in Fig. 12, changes in the set of environments weakly
1450   affect model performance on ME-Lotka-Volterra. For ME-Pendulum, however, the inclusion of
1451   each additional environment consistently improves model performance. On ME-SIREpidemic, the
1452   performance boost observed with "3 envs" underscores the critical role of the environment Enlarge.
1453   Two key observations regarding the ME-SIREpidemic are worth noting. First, the average perfor-
1454   mance degradation on "4 envs" suggests a reduced focus on the important environment Enlarge due
1455   to the addition of the final environment Loop. Second, the improvement in the best performance
1456   candidate demonstrates the additional benefits of the environment Loop. These findings illustrate
1457   that while adding environments can enhance the best possible discovery of invariant functions, it also
       increases the average training complexity that may cause average performance degradations.


                                                                                         27
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1458
       E.3     S YMBOLIC REGRESSION EXPLANATION COMPARISONS
1459
1460   To further evaluate the performance differences between the proposed method and the baselines,
1461   we apply PySR to the four baseline methods and obtain analytical explanations, as summarized
1462   in Tab. 7. The symbolic regression results provide an intuitive understanding of performance in
1463   relation to different NRMSE values. Specifically, when the NRMSE approaches 1, the resulting
1464   explanations are largely meaningless. As the NRMSE decreases to around 0.7, the explanations
1465   become more interpretable but may sometimes converge to oversimplified expressions, such as the
1466
       IRM and VREx results on ME-Lotka-Volterra. When the NRMSE approaches zero, the expressions
       become more reasonable but are not always ideal due to the inherent limitations of PySR. This
1467
       suggests that strong model performance does not necessarily guarantee high-quality explanations,
1468
       highlighting the performance constraints of the explainer (PySR).

1517

1518
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 Figure 14: A function learning example.
1519
1520   derivative function and therefore must run different derivative functions sequentially. Approaches
1521   like CoDA (Kirchmeyer et al., 2022) attempt to vectorize the model by employing group-based
1522   convolution networks. However, these module-based implementations rely on stateful PyTorch
1523   modules, requiring the derivative function module to be replicated during each forward pass, which
1524   slows down the process. While these Module-based implementations offer a slight improvement over
1525   non-vectorized methods due to the vectorization benefits, the performance gain is limited.
1526   In contrast, our vectorized Functional-based implementation leverages PyTorch’s functional methods,
1527   achieving 9.2x speedup by avoiding the overhead associated with stateful modules. Note that vector-
1528   izing hypernetworks using libraries such as hypnettorch (von Oswald et al., 2020) can deliver similar
1529   speedups. Finally, our Reference-based implementation, which eliminates parameter assignment
1530   after the first iteration, nearly doubles the forward pass speed (16.8x) compared to implementations
1531   that require such assignments. Notably, this optimization remains applicable for potential future
1532   CUDA-based parallel hypernetwork implementations.
1533
1534   G     S UPPLIMENTARY EXPLANATIONS
1535
1536
       The diagram 14 illustrates the conceptual function learning process associated with the DIF frame-
1537   work, exemplified by the prediction of dω    . Each (hidden) function representation, such as ẑc , ẑe , fˆc ,
                                                 dt
1538
       and fˆe , implicitly encodes both a symbolic function form and a value-assignment/substitution rule.
1539   For instance, fˆc represents two types of information: (1) the function form, e.g., −α2 sin(θt ), and (2)
1540   a partial assignment of values, such as {α → 1.5, θt →}, where α is assigned a value of 1.5 while θt
1541   remains a symbolic variable. This implies that fˆc represents the function −1.52 sin(θt ), which can
1542   accept input values in the form {θt →?}, effectively evaluated as fˆc (?). Similarly, fˆ is a function
1543   defined by two symbolic variables and accepts inputs in the form {θt →?, ωt →?}. In this specific
1544   example, the input to fˆ is {θt → 1.2, ωt → 0.4}, resulting in the evaluation fˆ(1.2, 0.4) = −2.23.
1545

1558
1559                Figure 15: Visualization of trajectory predictions on ME-Lotka-Volterra
1560   In this section, we present visualization comparisons for ME-Lotka-Volterra (Fig. 15) and ME-
1561   SIREpidemic (Fig. 16). The results for ME-SIREpidemic closely align with its quantitative findings.
1562   For the more challenging task of ME-Lotka-Volterra, our method’s predicted trajectories remain
1563   closer to the ground truth. In the X c predictions, where most methods fail, our predicted trajectory has
1564   turning points closest to the ground truth in terms of timing, although there are deviations in magnitude
1565   (Fig. 15b). The complexity of the ME-Lotka-Volterra task arises from several factors, including the
       introduction of exponential functions within environments, the distribution of environments, and


                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   29
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

1573
1574                Figure 16: Visualization of trajectory predictions on ME-SIREpidemic
1575
       the limited number of samples in each environment. Addressing these challenges requires carefully
1576   designed benchmarks by domain experts, which we will discuss further in the limitations section.
1577
1578
1579   I   L IMITATIONS AND F UTURE W ORK
1580
1581   We discuss the limitations and the cover scope of this work; thus, shedding light on potential future
1582
       directions accordingly.
1583   First, in this invariant function learning tasks, we only target the function combinations like f (x)+g(x)
1584   and f (x)(1+g(x)) = f (x)+f (x)g(x) where the invariant and the environment functions are entangled
1585   by additions. We ignore the scenarios that the invariant and environment functions are entangled in
1586   forms like multiplications. Therefore, it may be of interest to disentangle more complex scenarios.
1587   However, for physical systems, given proper derivatives, factors are generally combined by additions,
1588
       making the addition situations we considered important and widely applicable.
1589   Second, in this work, we only consider invariant function learning on ODE systems; thus, it will be
1590   of interest to explore invariant function in PDE systems.
1591
       Third, although we prove the invariant function learning principle using information theory, it would
1592   still be quite significant to further build invariant error bounds for this invariant function learning task
1593   using VC-Dimension Vapnik & Chervonenkis (2015) or PAC-Bayesian McAllester (1998); Catoni
1594   (2007), which would provide more in-depth insights.
1595
       Forth, in this work, we propose 4 multi-environment ODE systems, but we are still lacking com-
1596
       prehensive benchmarks that built by domain experts. There are many questions remaining, such as
1597
       how many environments are sufficient to infer invariant function, what are the requirements of the
1598   environment distributions to make it possible for invariant function learning.
1599
1600   Last but not least, unlike meta-parameters, the learned invariant functions have broader adaptation
1601
       capabilities. For instance, a learned physical law can generalize to any system. It would be fascinating
       to explore applications of invariant function learning, such as learning generalizable physics from
1602
       videos or developing physics-aware agents. Additionally, it would be interesting to investigate
1603
       whether invariant function learning can contribute to the development of foundational models in
1604   physics.
1605
1606
1607
1608
1609
1610
1611
1612

1614

1617

                                                           30
